Open Air, No ApologiesThe 1990 Mazda 121 Canvas Top — The Most Joyful Car That Nobody Expected

It doesn't take long. You reach for the handle, slide the canvas back, and the whole sky falls into your lap. This is the story of a car the market ignored, a boy in Hong Kong who found it, and why it still matters.

By Douglas Sellers · Carveyance · 9 min read
Full Specification — Mazda 121 Canvas Top (DA Series, 1990–1993)
Engine
1.3L B3 Inline-4 SOHC1,324cc · Carburetor · Gasoline
Power
67 hp @ 5,500 rpm76 lb-ft torque @ 3,000 rpm
0–62 mph
11.6 secondsFeels faster than it is
Top Speed
99 mph (159 km/h)One short of the milestone
Transmission
5-speed Manual4-speed automatic available
Drivetrain
Front-Wheel DriveIndependent suspension all round
Kerb Weight

Roof System

Cargo
190–420 litresSliding rear bench expands space
Fuel Economy
35 mpg combined29 city · 34 highway
Aerodynamics
Cd 0.36–0.40Nobody at Mazda lost sleep over it
Markets
Europe · Australia · HKSold as Autozam Revue in Japan

The year was 1989. Roadhouse, The Abyss, and Major League were in the theaters. Billy Joel, Cher, and the B-52's were topping the charts. The water gun company Entertech was in hot water for incidents where criminals were using their products to rob banks — the eighties were a wild time. While all of this was happening, my family had just moved back to Hong Kong for the third time.

The Pearl of Asia. Where east and west, old and new, city and gorgeous island landscape come together in harmony — a real yin and yang in the flesh. My father was in the market for a second car. Something small, fun, practical. Something to navigate the congested city with ease, yet a blast on the scenic island roads. He was set on a red Peugeot 205 GTI, and I could not have been more excited.

The 205 GTI, as you may or may not know, is a car people lust after. The presence alone — aggressive styling, staunch appearance — seemed to scream: "I'm more fun than a bachelor party in Monte Carlo with unlimited funds and party favors." OK, maybe not quite that fun, but a genuinely great time. With its front-wheel drive dynamics it would dominate and become legend on the rally circuit. It had that certain French smugness that says I am better than you — and it was right. So you can understand the excitement a young kid would have at the prospect of possibly driving it in a few years.

But alas. After hearing some horror stories about reliability and maintenance costs, my dad nixed the 205 GTI and my dreams were dashed. I was like a child losing a balloon at a fair, watching it whisk and float away in an almost taunting dance with no hope of ever retrieving it. It was a sad moment in time.

We ended up with the Mazda 121 LX Fun Top. At first glance, the Mazda had none of the Peugeot's grace, elegance, or flair. It just sat there like a dog — the kind of dog that when you threw a frisbee towards it, the frisbee would hit it in the face, and it would just keep giving you that dumb, idiotic smile right back at you. Think of the dog from the movie Up. It was ordinary. It was a happy smiling dog. And I wanted nothing to do with it.

Hiroshima, 1989 — The Beautiful Question

To understand what had arrived in our garage, you need to understand the extraordinary creative moment it came from. Hiroshima in the late 1980s was a place of remarkable energy. The Miata had just been revealed to a world that didn't know it needed it. The 323F was drawing admiring glances. Mazda's designers were in the middle of what they called their bio-design era — rounded forms, friendly proportions, shapes that looked less like machines and more like something that had grown rather than been engineered.

Into this atmosphere arrived a question that sounds almost naively simple: what if even the cheapest car could still feel joyful?

The answer was the 121 Canvas Top. Sold in Japan as the Autozam Revue — part of Mazda's experimental youth-focused sub-brand — and exported to Europe, Australia, and Hong Kong as the Mazda 121, it was a small, rounded, bubble-shaped hatchback with a party trick that no rival in its class had thought to offer: a full-length fabric roof that slid open on rails, transforming the entire top of the car into open air. Not a sunroof. Not a tilt-and-slide panel. The whole roof. From above the windscreen to above the rear seats, the sky became part of the cabin.

"What if even the cheapest car could still feel joyful?" — Mazda, Hiroshima, circa 1989

Mazda could have cut costs differently. They could have given it a cheaper interior. Instead they gave it a sliding roof that turned an economy hatchback into a conversation about what driving was supposed to feel like. Mazda takes more of a teacher's or coach's approach — they find the fun in learning and pass that knowledge on to the student, making the student more involved, more eager, more alive. The 121 was that philosophy made physical.

The Bubble That Fooled Everyone

When the 121 arrived in showrooms, it caused an immediate, affectionate stir. The nickname came immediately and has never left: the Bubble Car. The rounded flanks, the domed roof, the wide glassy eyes of its headlights — this was a car that looked like it had been drawn by someone who loved cars rather than someone who feared them. While every other supermini manufacturer in the early 1990s was chasing aerodynamic knife-edges and sharp creases, Mazda sent out something that looked like it wanted to be your friend.

Mazda did what they could with this conventional, fundamental boxy shape. Every possible edge was softened and rounded. The addition of blistered fenders comes as a striking surprise — typically a hallmark of high-performance machines with wide tires. To prevent a flat, uninspired profile, sculpted rocker panels introduce a sense of depth and dynamism. The design culminates in a gracefully sloping hatch that seamlessly flows into large, eye-catching taillights and a substantial rear bumper — delivering both visual balance and functional appeal.

And then there was the trick it pulled on expectations. The 121 was, by every external measure, a small car. At just 3.48 metres long and barely 1.6 metres wide, it was pocketable. But step inside and it confounded you completely. Headroom was extraordinary. The upright seating gave every occupant a commanding view. Four adults sat in it — comfortably — in a car that, from the outside, looked like it barely had room for two.

The Canvas Top — Engineering as Philosophy

The canvas roof mechanism of the 121 is one of those pieces of engineering that rewards the more you think about it. Mazda could have built a full convertible. They chose not to — deliberately, intelligently, and for reasons that make the Canvas Top a more interesting car than a convertible would have been.

A proper convertible would have required structural reinforcement along the sills and floor, adding weight and cost. It would have compromised the hatchback loading. It would have made the car harder to insure. So instead, Mazda's engineers kept the full body structure — roof rails, side pillars, all of it — and stretched a reinforced fabric panel between them, mounted on sliding rails. The structural rigidity was preserved. The hatch remained. The rear seats stayed full size. The boot still held a week's shopping.

The result could be operated in ways no ordinary sunroof could match. The canvas top slid front-to-rear, rear-to-front, or both directions simultaneously — opening the roof in configurations to suit the weather, the sun angle, the mood. On a warm morning you might crack it rearward to vent heat without wind. On a summer afternoon you'd slide the whole thing back and let the sky in from bonnet to boot. It was a convertible experience without the convertible compromise. And when it rained, you slid the canvas forward, and you were in an ordinary hatchback again — dry, warm, entirely normal.

"Open-air driving joy belongs to everyone — not just the people who can afford a sports car."

Without question, the canvas roof was the defining aspect of the car. The Peugeot was a better driver's car in every universe ever created or yet to be created. But it did not have a sliding roof. The entire roof disappeared with a touch of a button and you could have access to the world — and for a child, no car could do better. I would be standing on the back seat with friends, feeling the wind and screaming in pure ecstasy, something that only a few open-top SUVs could do but never to the same extent, because none of them had the dancing shoes of the 121.

The Secrets It Kept

After about a month, the car started to reveal its secrets — as one does in a relationship. If you show all your cards at once, you have nothing to build on. The 121 was no different. The more you engaged with it, the more it expressed itself, and the more it revealed the gem it had over the all-star prom king Peugeot.

The rear bench could slide forward and back on runners, and reclined. Let me say that again — the back seats were on a track and reclined in an entry-level economy car, so people in the back could feel like royalty. You would never find that in a modern car; it would not be cost-effective. The cargo space expanded from 190 to 420 litres at will — clever, simple, brilliantly practical, and something far more expensive cars were not offering.

The rear hinges would disengage from the floor and the entire seat would fold forward — giving you the room you would find in a modern mid-size SUV. In a car that weighed 1,708 pounds. These were not features you expected. These were not features you deserved at the price. These were gifts from engineers who believed that a small car should be taken seriously.

What It Felt Like to Drive

Here is the thing about light cars. The numbers don't tell you anything useful. Yes, 0–100 km/h in eleven seconds sounds slow. On paper, it is slow. But the experience of driving a car that weighs 775 kilograms is not the experience of driving a slow car. It is the experience of driving something alive.

With only 1,708 lbs it was much lighter on its feet, had poise, and was surprisingly agile in the turns. With its front-wheel drive and 5-speed manual, driving on the coastal mountain roads of Hong Kong — the ocean on one side, that roof open — I could not have been in a better car. Sure, a Miata would have been sublime. A Lotus Esprit would have been nirvana. Even the 205. But all of them were restricted in one way or another — practically, or in the lack of the openness to the world that the 121 offered. Think of it as the center of a Venn diagram. It has all the attributes of every circle.

Every input registered. Every corner asked a question and expected an answer. The steering communicated through your palms. The lightweight body changed direction with an alertness that cars costing five times as much had lost under their own mass. And above your head — if you'd slid the canvas back — the world was pouring in. Add the wind that doesn't buffet you because you're not going fast enough to be buffeted. It just moves through the cabin gently, carrying whatever the day smells like. Salt from the sea. Cut grass from a park you're passing. The city you live in, arriving through your roof.

The fact that it was a 121 meant no one ever gave it a second look — which I also enjoyed. It felt like I knew something no one else had figured out yet. In Hong Kong you could not drive that fast anyway, and I could push this car to its absolute limit thanks to its small 1.3-litre engine. It was never going to get ahead of me. This was the first time I started to understand the relationship between an owner and their vehicle. This was when I learned that you should always buy something that makes you smile.

"I have never kept a car this long. I can't let it go."

— Long-term 121 owner, after 14 years and 150,000 km

"I love my little car. It's the best car I ever had."

— Australian owner

These are not the words of people making excuses for a car. These are the words of people who fell in love with something and never quite recovered.

The Bio-Design Era — Mazda's Most Creative Moment

The MX-5 NA was born from the same philosophical soil as the 121. So was the 323F, the RX-7 FD, the rotary-engined AZ-1 microcar. This was a company that believed — genuinely believed — that the emotional experience of a car mattered as much as its specifications. The bio-design aesthetic meant rounded surfaces, friendly proportions, shapes drawn from nature rather than from wind-tunnel compromise. While every other manufacturer was chasing the lowest drag coefficient, the 121 sat at Cd 0.40 — virtually the worst in its class — and nobody at Mazda seems to have lost sleep over it. Because the car wasn't trying to be efficient. It was trying to be felt.

The Miata did this with two seats and a sports car price. The 121 did it with four seats, a hatchback boot, sliding rear bench, a full canvas roof, and the price of a modest holiday. That is not a lesser achievement. In many ways, it is a greater one. Most manufacturers design a car based on raw data — improvements from the previous generation, consumer inputs about cupholders. Not Mazda. Not with this car.

The Ones That Got Away — Goldy and Funtop

The 121 Canvas Top even spawned special editions that are, in retrospect, among the most charming automotive collaborations ever conceived. In Germany, Mazda partnered with Haribo — yes, the gummy bear company — to create the 121 'Goldy.' Painted gold, featuring canvas top panels printed with the iconic Haribo Gold Bear design, it was offered as a lottery prize. In 2022, a winning couple donated their Goldy to the Mazda Classic Automobile Museum Frey in Augsburg, where it sits today as one of the most genuinely delightful objects in automotive history.

In 1993, Mazda produced the 'Funtop' — just 300 units, with the folding canvas roof, alloy wheels, and a rear spoiler. Three hundred. If you find one, you keep it. These editions capture the spirit of the car perfectly. This was never a car that took itself seriously. It was a car that took fun seriously — which is an entirely different and considerably more admirable thing.

The Second Generation — When the Smile Went Away

A second generation was created, but it lost the charm and brilliance. It tried too hard to be a designer car. The three-door hatchback was replaced by an odd four-door compact that looked like a turtle from Super Mario Bros. The pop-top was there, but it was not as large, did not fit the design as well, and looked more like a bad toupee. The sliding back seats were gone. The useable trunk was replaced by a boot you could only fit airline-sized luggage into. The magic was gone. That happy dog had stopped smiling.

Why It Still Matters

Honest John, reviewing the 121 for its classics guide, made a prediction that has aged well: "Rare now, unmourned at the moment, but we suspect there could be a bit of a cult following in years to come." That following is here. The 121 Canvas Top is increasingly sought after by people who understand what it represents: a moment when a mainstream car manufacturer decided to build something that prioritised happiness over headlines.

The engines are legendarily durable. Multiple owners report 200,000 kilometres on original powertrains with routine maintenance. The mechanics are simple enough that any competent independent garage can work on them. What ages are the canvas mechanisms — stretching, cracking, the occasional leak in hard rain — but these are fixable, and the community of owners who have kept their cars on the road are generous with knowledge and parts.

I am living in Los Angeles now — one of the car meccas of the world. And I still crave this vehicle. I think it would be perfect here because of its uniqueness, practicality, fuel economy, and of course fun. I could park anywhere in the city, drive on the beach, throw anything in the back, and still have the convertible option. One could say I am biased because of where I was living at the time — and that could be true. But a good car is a good car anywhere.

The Carveyance Verdict
~800 kg of Pure Joy The Sky Belongs to Everyone

There is a very specific feeling this car gives you that no other car in its class, at its price, in its era has ever replicated. You slide the canvas back. The sky opens. The engine hums — quietly, honestly, without drama. The car weighs almost nothing and you feel every gram of that lightness in your fingertips. And all around you, the world is pouring in through the roof you slid away two minutes ago.

It is not fast. It is not prestigious. It will not frighten anything on the road. It will not impress anyone who hasn't driven one. But it will make you feel alive in traffic. It will make a Tuesday morning feel like a choice you made, rather than something that happened to you. It will make the person in the passenger seat turn to you — wind in their hair, sky above them in a car that cost the same as a second-hand sofa — and grin.

That is what the Mazda 121 Canvas Top was for. That is what it still does. The sky belongs to everyone.
